data.title: Rubrik: High Growth Rates And AI Resilience
data.description:
While it's undeniable that there is plenty of uncertainty in the air right now amid a shaky macroeconomy and the possibility of a drawn-out conflict in the Middle East, it's also rather plain to see that the selloff, particularly in software stocks, has gone too far. The "SaaSpocalypse" narrative that has hung like a dark cloud over software stocks this year has simply not been reflected in any near-term earnings data, and investors who have a strong stomach for the near term can take advantage of multi-year lows to buy high-quality businesses at a very attractive price.

Rubrik (RBRK), a cybersecurity-adjacent software company that specializes in backup and cyber resilience, is one of these deeply mispriced stocks. Shares are down ~30% since the start of January alone, wiping out all of last year's gains. On the heels of a strong Q4 earnings print and an upbeat outlook for FY27, it's a good time for investors to reassess the bull case in this name.

I last wrote a buy article on Rubrik in January, when the stock was still trading at $75 per share. At the time, I had called out the stock's immense growth rates and its rising free cash flow margin profile, with a note of caution on the company's premium valuation multiples. Since then, the company has continued to deliver upbeat fundamentals, but the "SaaSpocalypse" narrative has taken a huge bite out of its valuation multiples, though there is virtually no concrete evidence that AI poses any threat at all to its business (in fact, the rise of AI agents may even increase the need for its resilience products). I'm reiterating my buy rating here.

Let's kick off the discussion by quickly recapping what Rubrik does and how the AI selloff narrative fails to fit in here. Rubrik began selling off sharply in February, when Anthropic's Claude unveiled new security capabilities that made investors nervous about the entire cybersecurity space.

Rubrik slots in somewhat into the cybersecurity category, but its bread and butter isn't traditional firewalls and threat detection. Instead, Rubrik focuses on data resilience. The company's thesis is that the majority of cyberattacks don't come from hackers breaking into an enterprise system but simply logging in. The company's products focus on how to respond to events like this (while other cyber companies focus on how to prevent them). The company helps its customers back up their identity data and permissions to ensure minimum leakage and enable recovery of data assets in the event of an attack.

The key point that many investors may miss here is that Rubric's product involves a wide array of critical integrations to third-party software. Rubrik connects to firewall-style cybersecurity platforms like Palo Alto Networks (PANW) and Zscaler (ZS), but it also connects to enterprise software platforms like Salesforce (CRM) and data stored inside the likes of Azure and AWS to ensure businesses can remain active during a threat incident. It's difficult to imagine any company self-building this kind of tool using agentic AI or vibe coding and managing integrations to all of these various platforms without risking errors. I consider Rubrik's platform to be quite AI-resistant; in fact, I believe the rise of agentic AI that can perform tasks makes it even more critical that IT departments prepare for unwanted incidents.

Stay long here: none of Rubrik's recent results lend credence to the narrative that AI is a threat to its business.

Q4 Download

Let's now go through Rubrik's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Rubrik's revenue grew 46% y/y to $377.7 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $342.4 million (+33% y/y) by a huge thirteen-point margin. We have to call out upfront that a large chunk of Rubrik's recent revenue growth has been driven by the transition of its "material rights" deals. The company is phasing out selling hardware/software packages, and legacy customers received subscription credits instead of the hardware appliance refreshes they would have received in the past, which was recognized as additional subscription revenue.

The chart below, meanwhile, showcases the company's revenue normalized for this one-time tailwind, which added $7 million to Q4 revenue and $18 million to FY26 revenue (the benefit of this transition will fade in FY27, where the company is expecting only $10 million in revenue due to these material rights). We note that adjusted for this one-time revenue, Rubrik's normalized revenue would have grown at a 43% y/y pace, which is seven points stronger than 36% y/y in Q3; in fact, it was the best quarter all year. Again, we emphasize here: it looks like AI is a tailwind rather than a headwind to Rubrik's business.

We note as well that the company added $115 million of net-new subscription ARR within the quarter, growing its subscription ARR by 34% y/y to $1.46 billion.

The company's enlarged subscription base has also led to meaningful economies of scale. Pro forma gross margins rose 400 bps y/y to 83.7%, which skews very high even in the software industry.

Pro forma operating margins, meanwhile, improved to 1.6%. While still early on in its profit development, we note that Rubrik's pro forma operating margin improved 13 points versus -11.2% in the year-ago Q4.

Meanwhile, the company also delivered $237.8 million in free cash flow, rising 11x y/y and hitting an 18% margin that improved 16 points y/y.

Valuation, Risks, and Key Takeaways

At current share prices near $54, Rubrik trades at a $10.81 billion market cap. After we net off the $1.68 billion of cash and $1.13 billion of convertible debt on Rubrik's most recent balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $10.26 billion.

For FY27 (the year for Rubrik ending in January 2027), the company is guiding to total revenue of $1.597-$1.607 billion, which represents as-reported revenue growth of 21-22% y/y. The company notes that excluding the tough headwind to higher material rights revenue in FY26, true underlying growth would be 27-28% y/y. Management is also expecting $265-$275 million in FCF, or 17% FCF margins and 14% y/y FCF growth (considering FY26 FCF margin clocked in at 18%, there's likely room for upside here).

Rubrik now trades at:

  • 6.4x EV/FY27 revenue
  • 38x FY27 FCF

For a company expected to deliver just shy of 30% organic growth in FY27 alongside a burgeoning cash flow profile, Rubrik, to me, represents the epitome of "growth at a reasonable price."

Of course, there are also several risk factors that we need to watch out for. Cyber resilience may be a critical product to position in an IT stack, but it isn't as 100% critical as traditional firewalls. Amid a tough macro, IT budget pressures may prevail and push IT leaders to spend more of their budgets on enabling agentic AI operations rather than purchasing safeguards like Rubrik.

That said, we still find more positives than negatives in this stock, especially after this year's sharp slide has given us a more de-risked entry point at an attractive valuation. Buy the dip and stay long.

data.title: PagSeguro Digital: Brazil's Potential Rate Cuts And Credit Expansion Support Re-Rating
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered PagSeguro Digital (PAGS), I highlighted their strong top- and bottom-line growth, ramping-up their double-digit shareholder returns and showing their resilience while growing very well in payments and banking even during the ongoing macro pressures.

Following a solid report seen recently, PAGS remains a Strong Buy, backed by a very attractive valuation, a sustainable double-digit yield, and significant near- and long-term potential thanks to several tailwinds.

Internal Developments

PAGS reported a solid Q4 and 2025 as a whole, with a 16% YoY increase in revenue, of which 9% was in payments and a strong 51% in banking, while the net income grew at a more modest 4%, which is still an excellent performance given the ongoing macro pressure, all being either at the top end of the guidance or even above it if we're looking at the 20.6% EPS growth rate.

PAGS also released their 2026 guidance and confirmed the 2029 ambitions, expecting a very strong 25% to 35% growth rate this year in their credit portfolio, which could reach BRL 25 billion by the end of 2029, while the gross profit and EPS would grow at a CAGR of over 10% and 16% from 2025 to 2029, respectively, despite a slower 2026 due to macro uncertainty.

Financially, based on PAGS's latest report (note that this is in thousands of reais), we continue to see a very strong position, with their cash and cash equivalents doubling YoY to the equivalent of about US$357.7 million, while the current assets continue to cover their total liabilities, being in a very strong position overall, which I believe should allow them to keep expanding in the region.

PAGS returned roughly BRL 2.1 billion back to shareholders in 2025, which represents about 87.5% of their net income, which currently represents a whopping ~14.28% combined yield, which continues to be very strong. The current buyback program is ~80% complete now, and I expect them to renew it soon, likely even during the next meeting, with the company's CFO mentioning during their Q4 Earnings Call that they're likely to deploy the rest over the next months:

So we have the third buyback program that was launched last May. It remains open. we have been executing since then, approximately 80% of this buyback programs have been executed, and we're probably going to deploy the rest of this problem in the upcoming months.

Macro Developments

Probably the biggest macro development seen since comes from the expectations surrounding Brazil's SELIC rate (i.e., the benchmark rate set by the Central Bank of Brazil), with the market now expecting the following 7 policy meetings in 2026 to come with cuts of 50 basis points each after being maintained at the latest meeting.

This way, the SELIC rate would get to 11.5% by the end of 2026 compared to the current 15% rate, which can be amazing for a company like PAGS, which depends on economic activity, transaction volumes, new businesses, etc. The company also talked about this during their Q4 Earnings Call, while estimating it at a level between 12.5% to 13% by the end of 2026 and 10% for 2027:

When we talk about the SELIC rate, that's very important when we talk about the financial cost. When we look and what we expect for 2026, despite that we will face cuts in the interest rate along the year, the average SELIC probably is going to be quite close to the 2025 SELIC rate. And at the same time, we also assume that and included that in our 2026 guidance.

They also mentioned how big the impact of this rate is on their financing costs, which shows another layer of importance for the company, other than the natural slowdown effect it has on the consumer and businesses:

On the cost side, financial costs increased 39% year-over-year, driven mainly by the higher interest rate environment and the effects of recent capital structure adjustments as highlighted earlier. On the other hand, sequentially, financial costs reduced 1% due to the progress we have made in diversifying our funding structure and reducing our funding costs.

Not only that, but I mentioned before that all the ongoing US pressure and uncertainty can even lead to a migration of capital even into assets like this one, as the market could seek to diversify away from the country, while Brazil continues to offer several very high-yield opportunities, including PAGS.

The next SELIC decision is expected on March 18, but these expectations came before the ongoing Iran conflict, so it wouldn't be impossible to see some further delays until the situation potentially normalizes. Since Brazil is also a key oil and commodity player, this conflict can also cause potential disruptions, as the fundamentals of oil remain relatively unstable, with production growth being met by weak supply, both of which can be affected by the current high prices we see today.

This remains an ongoing matter, and even though I don't expect it to last for that long since an actual regime change is likely not the goal, every day spent at these prices can put even more pressure on an already weak global economy that was only recently starting to show some signs of improvement, potentially hurting PAGS' clients and the final consumer.

Risks Reiterated

Although there is potential to see improvements, there's certainly the possibility to see macro pressure continue, and given the ongoing Iran conflict and potential for things to escalate, we could even see delays in Brazil's rate cuts.

Since the company is ultimately tied to economic activity, this has the potential to cost them quite a bit, especially since they base their long-term estimates on lower rates, which remains an unpredictable topic, with potential delays likely resulting in the market "taxing" their valuation.

Valuation

For PAGS' DCF to Equity valuation, starting from their BRL 7.56 billion CFO, minus the BRL 2.28 billion in CAPEX and BRL 2.33 in net borrowings, getting us to an FCFE of roughly BRL 2.96 billion, getting us to a very strong US$570.5 billion in 2025.

Just to be extra conservative from the start and assume a CAGR of 12% until 2029, which is well below the 16% EPS CAGR they expect (they say this doesn't include buybacks, by the way), followed by a CAGR of 8% until year 10 and a 3% terminal growth rate given the region's potential, with a very high 15% discount rate to be extra careful given the region's and industry's risks, with 290.7 million shares outstanding as of their Q4 report.

Despite using conservative assumptions that are well below the company's near-term estimates and potential, we get to an equity value of $7.65 billion, for an intrinsic value of US$26.30 per share, which is significantly above the current US$9.98 at the moment of writing this, leaving a massive margin of safety.

Conclusion

PAGS remains a Strong Buy, backed by significant undervaluation and very strong potential to grow in LATAM, especially if domestic tailwinds advance as expected, with sustainable double-digit returns on top.

There are risks to keep in mind given the competition or even the region itself, but the overall risk-reward remains significantly in their favor, in my opinion, and PAGS remains a position that I consider increasing on a regular basis.

data.title: Arista Networks: Why A Breakout Seems Likely (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Back in November, I upgraded Arista Networks, Inc. (ANET) from a sell to a hold rating. I remained relatively cautious on the stock, as the business seemed supply constrained and the valuation was deemed to have been fair. As shown in the rating history chart below, Arista Networks has had very mild gains since then. Today, I will evaluate their most recent earnings reported in mid-February as well as assess how some recent developments are affecting their outlook. The goal today is to provide readers with an updated perspective and see if the stock has become a buy in my eyes.

In the below analysis, it is shown that Arista Networks continues to be supply constrained as purchase commitment growth far outpaces revenue increases. While this risk may have played a factor in their 2026 guidance, the company has a massive opportunity at hand, and recent developments show that the likelihood of them capturing a good part of the TAM is high. Therefore, the valuation contraction since my previous article has created a buying opportunity in my view.

TAM Continues To Grow

Before looking specifically at their 2025 Q4 financial results, let's take a big step back and briefly discuss the multiyear opportunity at hand. As you can see in the infographic above, their TAM expanded notably in 2024 from 2023. However, that is a relatively small increase when compared to 2025's annual expansion. TAM is now $105 billion, and while their Core business is taking the spotlight due to the AI boom, their Strategic Expansion and Cognitive Networks businesses also have significant room to grow over the next few years. What investors should take away from this is that the opportunities for Arista Networks are expanding at an accelerating pace. Now, it's likely a matter of execution by management to see how much of the market they can capture.

Growth Ticks Up

For their 2025 Q4 results, let's start with a look at top-line activity and demand trends. In the fourth quarter, Arista Networks generated revenues of $2.5 billion, and that represented a respectable growth rate of 28.9% YoY. This was a mild acceleration from Q3's 27.5% increase, and so there has been a slight increase in business momentum. One of the more encouraging signs for the top line in Q4 was that all three of their product sectors helped to power growth as a sign of broad strength. The company also managed to beat analyst expectations by nearly $100 million, and so, judging by these results, Arista Networks has seen reasonably robust activity as the data center buildout in the U.S. and other countries continues.

For a clear indicator of where demand is, let's take a look at the increase in purchase commitments. QoQ, you can see on the right that there has been a 42% increase. That is a major jump from just the previous quarter and shows that essentially the lineup for Arista Networks' products is getting longer. Note that in QoQ terms, revenues grew by just 7.8%. Therefore, purchase commitment growth is far outpacing the top line. This is usually a clear sign of rock-solid demand. It should also be mentioned here that their deferred revenue grew by 14.6% QoQ, and so overall it seems that supply constraints continue to be a factor that is limiting Arista Networks' growth at this point. This is probably the biggest risk to their business right now.

Margins Are Mixed

With demand being so strong, it was a bit of a surprise for me that their gross margin actually contracted. However, this is probably not a major concern at this point. In their earnings presentation, the company mentioned that Q4's 63.4% is still within their historic range and that "a higher concentration of Cloud & AI Titan sales" is reflected in their gross margin figure. Until there is a more material deterioration that is driven by competitive pressures or substantial cost headwinds, I wouldn't draw the conclusion that profitability is getting eroded.

YoY, Arista Networks' operating expenses increased from $332 million to $397 million as they continued to make strategic investments in areas like liquid cooling and high-radix switching. With innovation being a competitive advantage of theirs, this spending is justified. Furthermore, as a percentage of revenues, operating expenses actually declined from 17.2% to 16%, and that is clearly reflected in the operating margin figure. While gross margin was a bit unfavorable, strong operational efficiency helped to more than offset that, as operating margin actually expanded by 50 basis points. Therefore, core profitability of the company is in a good position currently.

On the bottom line, growth did accelerate for EPS, and they were also able to beat expectations by $0.06. While the 26% increase to $0.82 actually underperformed the top line growth rate to show mixed profitability, I believe investors should mainly focus on the operating margin number above for a clearer picture of where true operating profitability is.

Outlook & Opportunities

The company provided both quarterly and annual guidance. We'll focus on the full year 2026 numbers here, but it should be mentioned that they guided Q1 revenues above expectations. The annual outlook is shown above, and you can see that they raised their guidance for some key metrics. For top line growth, they now see around 25%. This is contrasted with a 28.6% increase for the full year 2025, and so there is some momentum slowdown expected. As stated earlier, the risk of supply constraints is much more noteworthy than demand worries at this point. It seems that this revenue estimate implies that supply will continue to be an issue moving forward.

As for profitability, both gross margin and operating margin are set for contraction relative to 2025 if the targets laid out above are met. Therefore, operating efficiency may not come to the rescue like it did in Q4. Overall, despite their raising of some expectations, I'm a bit disappointed with this near-term outlook, as at the end of the day both business activity and profitability are set to soften from what we saw in the past year.

After their earnings release, there have been some developments that show that the company does have good opportunities and growth drivers. In late February, AMD (AMD) and Meta (META) announced what was described as a megadeal. This is a clear indication that the AI infrastructure buildout is set to continue. With Arista Networks being a key supplier of infrastructure solutions, the company is set to see tailwinds from this deal specifically and from continued capital spending by hyperscalers in general. In my view, this shows that the long-term outlook remains solid.

Another significant development is that just today (Mar 12th), Arista Networks announced their XPO High Density Liquid Cooled Pluggable Optics. I believe this is a demonstration of their innovative capabilities. Chief Architect Andreas Bechtolsheim stated that,

The unprecedented growth in AI fabric bandwidth and the transition to liquid cooling requires a new generation of pluggable optics modules. XPO solves this challenge by providing fundamental improvements in density, cooling capability and reliability for pluggable optics modules.

The release of such technologies shows that the company's commitment to R&D is justified and that they remain in position to capture a good part of the market. As mentioned earlier in the TAM discussion, the opportunity's there, and now it's a matter of execution. I would say that despite the supply constraints and softer than desired 2026 guidance, Arista Networks' positioning is quite strong for the long term.

Valuation A Bit More Attractive

Despite some ups and downs, the forward P/E ratio has come down from the 43 reading seen in my previous article. While the current level of 38.41 for the multiple isn't the cheapest seen in the market today, the potential for high levels of growth over the medium to long term makes the stock attractive.

In today's analysis, we learned quite a bit about where the fundamentals and outlook of Arista Networks are at. Firstly, demand continues to be through the roof as purchase commitments soar. Judging by their Q4 revenue and 2026 guidance, supply remains the top-of-mind issue for the company. Over time, I expect bottlenecks to ease somewhat, and so, given that the company is a leader in AI infrastructure solutions, there is a good chance that they will be able to capture a significant part of the TAM that they outlined. In my view, the company may be dealing with some near-term challenges, but the long-term outlook has continued to strengthen.

In my previous article, I concluded that the valuation was more or less fair. With the long-term prospects further brightening since then and the forward P/E ratio having come down a bit, I believe the stock is now in a position where the risk/reward is favorable for investors.

Given that their revenue for the full year 2025 was around $9 billion but the TAM is now estimated to be above $100 billion, you can see that the potential for growth is immense. As stated above, Arista Networks is in a position to capitalize on the opportunities at hand. Therefore, while the forward P/E of 38 doesn't seem particularly low using forward 1-year analyst estimates, on a multiyear timeframe the multiple would drop to irrationally low levels if the stock price doesn't rise from here.

Final Thoughts

After the November selloff, the stock has been mostly stuck in an extended period of consolidation. I believe there is a good chance that this will end with a new leg up for Arista Networks. The combination of an expanding TAM, innovative products, and a pullback in the valuation sets the stock up for outperformance even though near-term supply challenges persist. As a result, I've decided to upgrade Arista Networks to a buy rating for the first time.

data.title: Petco Health And Wellness Company's Turnaround Has Officially Started
data.description:
March 12th was a fantastic day for shareholders of Petco Health and Wellness Company (WOOF). Shares traded up 34.6% for the day. This surge came after management announced financial results for the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. After seeing years of revenue declines, as well as mixed financial performance when it came to profits and cash flows, the worst for the business looks like it might be over. Although earnings per share fell short of what analysts were anticipating, revenue exceeded expectations. More importantly, management is pointing toward a potential for revenue growth this year, with overall profitability expected to rise as well.

This is great because, since I last wrote about the business back in March of 2023, the stock has taken a huge dive. Even after this increase in share price, shares are down 68.6%, while the S&P 500 is up 64.9%. Truly, it has been a painful couple of years for investors. But if the worst is over, and when we add on top of this the fact that shares are incredibly cheap on both an absolute basis and relative to similar companies, then upside potential becomes justified. In the near term, I wouldn't be surprised to see its share price pull back. That is common in cases like this. But for those focused on the long haul, this might be the pivotal moment where the turnaround is finally coming to fruition. Given how cheap the stock is, I think that the risk profile for investors is quite low. Because of this, I believe that rating it a soft and speculative ‘buy’ is the right move here.

Some Great News At Last!

Over the last few years now, the picture for shareholders of Petco Health and Wellness Company has been bleak. As an example of this, we need only look at the chart above. Revenue fell year after year, dropping from $6.26 billion in 2023 to $5.96 billion in 2025. These 2025 figures are new, having just been released by management. According to the company, the overall drop in sales has been driven by soft discretionary spending and the shifting of consumer preferences toward value-centric products caused by broader economic turbulence.

Truly, these have been trying times for the average consumer in the US. The COVID-19 pandemic created significant inflationary pressures. There were other factors as well, such as a surge in spending by both the Donald Trump and Joe Biden administrations. The Federal Reserve ultimately had to hike interest rates. And that made affordability issues even more challenging. And then, just as things were starting to look up, tariffs came into play, serving as a massive regressive tax for the American consumer. The end result of all of this was a plunge in consumer confidence levels. In the chart above, you can see precisely what I mean here. The latest jobs data is also discouraging, pointing toward a very likely recession. And unfortunately, even though many pet owners like myself view their pets as members of their family, they aren't afraid to make certain cost cuts when economic times are challenging.

Profitability for the company has been all over the map. Some of this is because of certain one-time events. But regardless, as the first chart in this article illustrates, the overall picture has been rough at best. The good news is that 2025 at least showed some improvement, with every single profitability metric that I looked at for the company showing growth year over year.

The newest data provided by management covered the final quarter of the year. At first glance, it may not look all that impressive. Revenue, as an example, came in at only $1.52 billion. That was down from the $1.55 billion that the company reported a year earlier. Most of this drop was because of a 1.6% decline in comparable sales. The good news is that a lot of this decline was driven, according to the company, by management's decision to move away from unprofitable sales and to also shutter underperforming locations. Even though revenue did drop, it did come in about $1.8 million above what analysts were hoping to see.

On the bottom line, the picture was more nuanced. The company saw its loss per share decline from $0.05 to $0.01. That still resulted in management missing expectations to the tune of $0.02. But at least it still meant an improvement in the bottom line from a net loss of $13.8 million to a net loss of $2.6 million. Clearly, cutting out unprofitable sales and closing underperforming stores is working for the company. And the end result has been not just higher profits but higher cash flows as well. Operating cash flow jumped from $96 million to $153.6 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we get an improvement from $91.5 million to $168 million. And finally, EBITDA for the company rose from $96.1 million to $106.3 million.

This is fine and dandy. But the real story here is what management expects moving forward. The firm is pushing its ‘Reach for the Sky’ strategy, which it defines as the third phase of its turnaround plan. You see, after strengthening its operating and economic model and completing the buildout of its senior leadership team with seasoned retail executives, and after focusing on merchandising excellence, improving the customer experience, achieving operational efficiencies, and boosting productivity for the services that they offer, the company is now on what it describes as the offensive. This is centered around long-term revenue growth and profit increases. This seems to be dedicated to improving the company's competitive position, driving operational improvements at its locations, expanding its online experiences, and making additional investments aimed at making its services top of the line.

Management highlighted four pillars of their current economic model. The first of these is to offer a regular stream of product newness that, when combined with its evergreen assortment of offerings, keeps it at the forefront of customers’ awareness. It involves creating memorable and differentiated in-store experiences for its customers that are centered around championing pet parenthood. Another pillar involves ensuring that it continues to employ a national network of groomers, trainers, veterinarians, and pet professionals. And the final part of this is to enhance its digital capabilities that involve a revamped membership offering, pushing for repeat orders and deliveries, and creating what management described as a seamless digital-to-store engagement.

Using these pillars, the company intends to return to growth. But it's not just any growth. It's profitable growth. For 2026 as a whole, it is possible that revenue could be flat. However, the higher end of the revenue range is a sales increase of 1.5%. Even more impressive is the expectation that EBITDA should grow from $408.2 million last year to between $415 million and $430 million this year. If management achieves this, it would mean that the company has successfully grown cash flows two years in a row now. And in all likelihood, hitting the midpoint of the target for EBITDA would mean an increase in adjusted operating cash flow from $240.2 million last year to about $297.5 million this year.

With these estimates, we can see in the chart above how shares of Petco Health and Wellness Company are valued right now. Honestly, these are very low multiples. Leading up to this point, they reflected a company that seemed to be in a perpetual state of decline. But that is no longer the case if management is correct about the future that the company offers. In that same chart, I compared it to five other pet-oriented businesses. What I found was that, on a price-to-operating cash flow basis, it ended up being the cheapest of the group. Meanwhile, on an EV-to-EBITDA basis, only one such firm was cheaper than it is.

Takeaway

Based on the data that's currently available, it is my assertion that Petco Health and Wellness Company serves as a compelling, if still risky, prospect. I actually believe that 2026 will see the US enter into a recession. And if I am correct, I believe that the guidance provided by management will end up being revised lower. But as long as we don't see a collapse in fundamentals, and as long as the nuts and bolts are there to ensure long-term success, I believe that the picture is attractive enough to justify rating it a soft and speculative ‘buy.’

data.title: PGIM Jennison Global Infrastructure Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

The S&P Global Infrastructure Index advanced 2.2% in the fourth quarter. Benchmark performance was led higher by solid performance in the utility and transportation infrastructure sectors. The midstream energy segment was the only notable area of index weakness during the period.

Quarter

The PGIM Jennison Global Infrastructure Fund advanced but modestly underperformed the S&P Global Infrastructure Index for the quarter.

In absolute terms, the Fund was led by strong performance from Ferrovial (FER), selected names in the Utility sector (GREENERGY RENOVABLES, Elia Group, Italgas) and the transportation company Group Aeroportuario del Pacifico. A number of Utilities also underperformed during the period, such as Vistra, Southern Company (SO), Xcel Energy (XEL), as did Cheniere Energy (LNG).

Relative to the S&P Global Infrastructure Index, the Fund's positioning in transportation infrastructure detracted the most from relative results. In addition, out of index exposure to communications and information technology hurt relative performance. On the upside, the Fund's positioning in midstream energy and utilities benefited relative performance during the period.

Key Contributors

  • Ferrovial
  • Grenergy Renovables
  • Elia Group

Ferrovial , the Spain-based infrastructure and construction company, maintained its strong momentum through the fourth quarter, supported by resilient performance across its core toll road portfolio and continued strength in North American traffic volumes. Revenue growth remained robust, aided by a strong double digit price increase announced for its primary Canadian concession in 2026. Additionally, Ferrovial will be included in the Nasdaq 100 adding to buying momentum in the name.

Grenergy Renovables , a Madrid-based renewable energy company, was a notable contributor during the quarter as investor sentiment toward European renewable energy operators improved following easing bond yields and supportive regulatory developments. The company advanced several key solar and wind projects to late-stage development and reported stable power purchase agreement pricing, which bolstered revenue visibility heading into 2026. Operational execution across its energy generation and services segments contributed to earnings resilience despite macroeconomic volatility.

Elia Group operates as a leading European transmission system operator in Belgium and Germany. The company continued to deliver sector-leading growth during the fourth quarter, with ongoing investment in cross-border grid expansion and offshore wind interconnections. Execution on its multi-year capital plan remained on track, and regulatory clarity around cost recovery further strengthened investor confidence. The stock performed well as markets recognized the growing importance of regulated transmission infrastructure in enabling Europe's energy transition. With a proven track record of delivering complex grid projects and a visible growth pipeline, Elia remains well positioned as a high-quality compounder within the regulated utility space.

Key Detractors

  • Cheniere Energy
  • Vistra Corp. (VST)
  • NEXTDC Limited (NXDCF)

Cheniere Energy is an integrated, full-service liquefied natural gas (LNG) company that owns and operates LNG receiving terminals and pipelines in Louisiana and Texas. Shares lagged during the fourth quarter as natural gas prices softened and investor sentiment across the broader energy complex weakened amid milder winter weather forecasts and elevated storage levels. Although quarterly results remained broadly in line with expectations, the company's reaffirmed full-year guidance and strong long-term contract portfolio offered limited offset to near-term commodity-driven volatility. The market's focus on declining spot margins overshadowed continued expansion progress at the Corpus Christi Stage 3 project. We continue to view the company's extensive, long-dated contract base as a durable cash flow anchor supporting long-term value creation through the next LNG investment cycle.

Vistra Corp. is an independent power producer operating an integrated retail and electric power generation business primarily in markets throughout the U.S. Shares underperformed in the quarter as sentiment within independent power producers softened, driven by investor caution around the timing of data center procurement activity and near-term power market volatility. While earnings and free cash flow guidance remained consistent with expectations, the stock experienced pressure following reduced forward price expectations across select regional power markets. Despite these headwinds, Vistra's diversified generation portfolio, continued strength in retail load, and exposure to structural Artificial Intelligence (AI)-driven electricity demand underpin its long-term growth profile.

NEXTDC Limited develops and operates hyperscale data centers across Australia and the Asia-Pacific region. Shares detracted from performance as the regional technology sector experienced valuation compression amid macroeconomic pressures and cautious investor sentiment toward capital-intensive digital infrastructure. Despite reporting strong operational metrics, including record utilization rates and continued pre-leasing momentum, rising financing costs weighed on near-term equity performance. The company remains well positioned to benefit from sustained hyperscale and cloud spending across the Asia-Pacific region, and its strategic focus on energy-efficient, renewable-backed data center design aligns closely with structural themes driving long-term digital infrastructure demand.

Portfolio Outlook

Overall, listed infrastructure stocks declined modestly through December 2025, though the asset class continued to outperform the broader markets as its defensive characteristics offset a weak market backdrop. The strategic importance of reliable baseload power capacity remained a central theme as hyperscale data center projects continued to advance, but investors rotated selectively within the universe following a strong year-to-date run in several power-related segments. Electric utility and power generation exposures remained broadly resilient relative to benchmarks, supported by structural demand growth and differentiated positioning in grid-constrained regions, while areas tied more directly to macro-sensitive growth and higher-beta risk factors experienced greater valuation pressure.

European infrastructure shares remained comparatively resilient in December, aided by supportive regulatory frameworks and continued visibility on long-term capital investment programs. Construction and engineering-focused names benefitted from solid backlogs and disciplined project execution, even as new order momentum moderated amid softer macro data. Regulated utilities in the region again provided ballast to returns, with transmission and multi-utility operators outperforming their global peers thanks to strong balance sheets and clear pathways for grid modernization and renewable integration. Transport infrastructure performance remained mixed; toll roads and rail assets delivered steady cash flows, while airport operators faced persistent cost pressures and uneven passenger mix recovery.

In the United States, power generation and utility sectors underperformed in absolute terms during December but continued to fare better than the broader infrastructure complex as the “AI power” theme shifted from multiple expansion toward an earnings- and project-execution-driven phase. The structural step-up in electricity demand remained intact, yet profit-taking and increased scrutiny of project timing, regulatory approvals, and customer cost pass-through introduced greater share price volatility. Transmission-focused and grid-centric business models were generally favored over more complex vertically integrated structures facing heightened regulatory and political attention around affordability and reliability.

North American natural gas infrastructure remained in recalibration mode as markets reassessed medium-term demand and capacity needs following a volatile year for commodity prices and policy debate. Pipeline and midstream systems continued to move robust volumes under long-term contracts, but equity valuations reflected questions about the pace and scale of future expansion projects and the permitting environment for new long-distance infrastructure. Gas-fired generation retained its critical role balancing intermittent renewables and fast-growing data center load, reinforcing the fuel's position as a key component of the evolving power mix even as investors increasingly differentiate among regions and asset quality.

Electric utility and power generation segments experienced some multiple compression into year-end, yet remained supported by visibility on large, regulator-backed capital expenditure plans for transmission, distribution, and interconnection upgrades. Companies with strong balance sheets, proven execution on complex grid projects, and meaningful exposure to high-growth demand corridors continued to be viewed as central beneficiaries of the power infrastructure “super-cycle”. In contrast, communications-oriented infrastructure linked to towers and data centers within the wider listed universe saw persistent rate-sensitive de-rating, even as data traffic and cloud workloads continued to rise.

Transport infrastructure fundamentals stayed broadly intact despite softer macro data and ongoing trade and geopolitical uncertainty. North American toll roads demonstrated solid traffic volumes and pricing power, reflecting resilient commuting and freight patterns, while freight and passenger rail assets benefited from stable long-haul demand and incremental efficiency gains. In Europe, sustained public investment and policy support underpinned long-term capital programs across key corridors, though airport-linked assets remained more volatile due to fluctuating travel patterns, labor constraints, and elevated operating costs.

Transport infrastructure fundamentals showed resilient performance despite continued uncertainty from evolving trade policies. North American toll roads continued benefiting from solid traffic volumes and pricing power, while European transport infrastructure companies remained supported by fiscal spending commitments across major economies. Airport operators experienced divergent performance with recovery patterns varying by region and operational focus, though overall trends remain constructive for companies with strong market positions.

Communications infrastructure across global markets continued to contend with higher real yields and elevated capital intensity, which weighed on valuations for tower and data-center-heavy business models. Nevertheless, structural drivers—including 5G densification, cloud adoption, and AI-related compute requirements—remained firmly in place, pointing to ongoing needs for densified networks and high-availability interconnection hubs. Markets increasingly demanded clearer line-of-sight to free-cash-flow growth, disciplined capital allocation, and potential capital returns before re-rating these assets, leaving the segment's near-term performance more sensitive to macro and policy shifts than other parts of the infrastructure universe.

data.title: Retreating But Not Defeated: AUD/USD Bulls Find Hope In Technical Support And Hawkish RBA
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

AUD/USD fell 1%+ on Thursday, with the pair reacting to a resurgent US dollar as safe haven demand intensified due to Middle East tensions. AUD/USD was trading at a multi-year peak, and thus a retracement may be welcomed in some quarters.

Hawkish RBA may limit downside

Inflationary pressures are intensifying in Australia, where the Melbourne Institute survey recently reported that March Consumer Inflation Expectations climbed to 5.2%, the highest level since July 2023.

This uptick from February's 5% reading underscores the growing challenge for the Reserve Bank of Australia (RBA), which had already taken a proactive stance in early February by raising the Official Cash Rate (OCR) by 25 basis points to 3.85%.

As the ongoing energy crisis continues to drive costs higher, market speculation is mounting that the RBA and other global central banks will be forced to maintain an aggressive interest rate hiking cycle to contain burgeoning prices.

According to the latest LSEG data, markets are pricing in around a 78% probability of a 25 bps rate hike at the upcoming March 17, 2026 meeting.

Economic data ahead

While the Australian economic calendar remains quiet this Friday, the focus shifts to a heavy slate of data from the United States. Markets are bracing for a series of high-impact releases, including the January Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) Price Index, the Federal Reserve’s preferred inflation metric, alongside January Durable Goods Orders.

Additionally, the preliminary March reading of the Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index will provide a fresh look at how the spike in energy prices and financial market volatility are impacting American consumer confidence.

Markets may remain at the mercy of geopolitical developments, which could even overshadow tomorrow's US data. The intriguing part about AUD/USD is the technical picture, where the pair is at a key inflection point.

Technical Analysis - AUD/USD

From a technical point of view, AUD/USD has finally pulled back from its multi-year peak around the 0.7187 handle. The pullback has brought the pair to a key inflection area resting between the 0.07072 (100-day MA) and 0.7051 (200-day MA) handles.

The previous impulse move to the upside occurred after a breakout of the 0.7034 handle, which rests just below the inflection area.

AUD/USD Four-Hour Chart, March 13, 2026

Dropping down to a one-hour chart, there is some evidence that bulls may already be returning. The price has slowed significantly as it approached the 0.7070 handle.

The bigger hint comes from the period-14 RSI, which has just left oversold territory. This is usually seen as a sign that momentum has shifted in favor of the bulls.

An immediate hurdle on the H1 chart rests at 0.7100. Acceptance back above this level is needed before the 0.7130 and recent highs at 0.7187 come into focus.

data.title: Harbor Diversified International All Cap Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
We are beginning to see signs that the unprecedented global tightening cycle of the past few years is impacting companies. - Marathon London

Market in Review

The fourth quarter started with relative calm, as most global markets trended sideways until the final week of November. In the previous week, a downswing had been in place, but the trend reversed — and the upswing — comparatively gentle though it was, continued unchecked into year-end. All regional MSCI indexes that Marathon follows ended the period ahead, with the MSCI All Country World Index (ACWI) returning 3.3%. For the first time in several years, the U.S. market was a relative laggard; the regions were led by Europe, with the MSCI Europe Index generating a return of 6.2%.

The quarter's initial uncertainty was caused, at least partly, by the U.S. government shutdown, which occurred from the first day of the period until November 12. This event led to the closure of various government departments that provide economic data upon which many investors base their trading decisions. Following the resumption of services, markets began to worry that data would not be reliable enough to support their positions, and the market declined until the first official releases showed a benign picture, allowing the U.S. Federal Reserve to cut rates twice during the quarter.

By style, quality outperformed both value and growth globally. Regionally, that was only true in the U.S. In all other regions value outperformed growth by between 3% and 6%. By size, large-cap stocks outperformed in all regions.

Retirement Class shares commenced operations on March 1, 2016. The performance attributed to the Retirement Class shares prior to that date is that of the Institutional Class shares. Performance prior to March 1, 2016 has not been adjusted to reflect the lower expenses of Retirement Class shares. During this period, Retirement Class shares would have had returns similar to, but somewhat higher than, Institutional Class shares due to the fact that Retirement Class shares represent interests in the same portfolio as Institutional Class shares but are subject to lower expenses.

Expense ratio information is as of the Fund's current prospectus, as supplemented. Gross expenses are the Fund's total annual operating expenses.

The net expense ratios for this fund are subject to a contractual management fee waiver and/or expense limitation agreement, excluding interest expense and acquired fund fees and expenses (if any), through 02/28/2026.

Portfolio Performance

During the fourth quarter, the Harbor Diversified International All Cap Fund (Institutional Class, "Fund") returned 3.31%, underperforming its benchmark, the MSCI All Country World Ex.-U.S. (ND) Index, which returned 5.05%. We believe the Fund's underperformance was a result of stock selection in Europe, though it also lagged slightly in the Pacific and Canada.

From a sector perspective, underperformance was equally split between stock selection and asset allocation, which is a result of stock selection decisions rather than a separate decision within the investment process.

Stock selection was strong in Materials but lagged in Industrials and Health Care. Similarly, allocation impacts were generally minor, but the underweight to Information Technology was materially negative. Only the underweight to Communication Services partially mitigated the impact. From a style perspective, value outperformed growth, which was beneficial in the Pacific, but less so elsewhere. Large-cap stocks outperformed small- and mid-cap stocks.

Contributors & Detractors

Leading detractors during the quarter included 3i Group (IIIYY), Roche (RHHBY), (not holding) SK Hynix (HXSCL), Tencent Music Entertainment (TME), and Naspers.

In November, 3i Group reported earnings in line with expectations, but warned that the outlook for key markets — particularly France, 3i's subsidiary Action's largest market — was deteriorating, which triggered a sharp sell-off; the stock fell around 25% from its previous quarterly peak.

Roche outperformed during the quarter, thus the Fund's underweight to the stock was a negative for relative performance.

Korea's SK Hynix, a semiconductor company with a focus on high-speed memory, soared amid demand from AI data centers. Our lack of exposure, therefore, negatively impacted relative performance.

The Chinese market fell sharply during the quarter, thus holding Tencent (TCEHY) Music (a listed subsidiary of Tencent) was a negative. Similarly, South Africa-listed Naspers, which is an investor in a variety of technology companies and notably the largest indirect holder of Tencent shares, suffered.

Top contributors during the quarter included Barclays (BCS), Standard Chartered (SCBFY), Capitec Bank, and (not holding) Alibaba Group (BABA) and Tencent Holdings.

Positions in the U.K.'s Barclays and Standard Chartered appreciated as investors rotated into value stocks. Despite strong gains in recent years, European banks remain conservatively valued versus many non-European peers.

South Africa's Capitec Bank reported a 26% increase in earnings (local currency terms) on the first day of the quarter, driven by an expansion of its banking client base, insurance business, and fintech arm.

The Chinese market fell sharply, led by technology and electric vehicle stocks. The Fund's lack of exposure to social media giant Tencent and online retailer Alibaba thus benefited performance.

Buys and Sells

The Fund acquired Iberdrola (IBDRY), Scout24 (SCOTY), and Empresa Nacional de Telecomunicaciones (ENTUY).

Spain's Iberdrola, a leading electric utility, is diversified across the U.S., U.K., Spain, and Brazil. The firm owns natural monopoly assets in networks where new capital cannot enter due to regulation.

Scout24, Germany's leading online property portal, is a technology-led firm with recent double-digit growth. After a rally earlier in the year, shares were down 30% from their high at the time of purchase, presenting Marathon with an attractive entry point.

Empresa Nacional de Telecomunicaciones offers telecommunications services to corporate clients in Chile. Forthcoming consolidation creates scope and the opportunity for pricing power to grow.

We exited positions in Amrize, M3, and Gruma (GPAGY).

Amrize, a holding inherited from a spin-off from Swiss stock Holcim (HCMLY) in June, was sold. The firm's revenue is derived mostly from the U.S., and the stock was moved to the U.S. index in November.

M3 supplies online medical information services for doctors and supports marketing of pharmaceutical companies and medical equipment manufacturers. The stock outperformed meaningfully, and the valuation had risen significantly over the past year until the time of Marathon's exit.

Gruma is engaged primarily in the production, marketing, distribution, and sale of tortillas, corn flour, and wheat flour. The tortilla industry capital cycle appears to be deteriorating with an increasingly cost-conscious consumer down-trading to private labels from core brands like Gruma, resulting in an increasingly aggressive pricing environment.

Overweights and Underweights

The Fund's sector weightings are a by-product of Marathon London's bottom-up stock selection process and do not change materially over the course of a quarter or year.

As of December 31, 2025, Industrials and Consumer Staples were the largest sector overweights, sitting at 5.5% and 2.7%, respectively; Information Technology was still the largest underweight at -6.4%, now followed by Health Care at -3.3%.

Country Allocations

The Fund's exposure to developing markets has not changed materially during the quarter. As of December 31, 2025, the U.K. and South Africa remained the most overweight positions at 8.5% and 3.5%, respectively; China is still the largest underweight at -4.0%, now followed by Switzerland at -4.0%.

Outlook

Marathon London's approach is long term, with a typical target holding period of more than five years. Portfolio managers do not take short-term positions, and Marathon London does not undertake any macroeconomic research, have "house views," or make any such predictions or forecasts.

That said, one interesting observation has been the effect of the extended ultra-low interest rates on Marathon London's approach. Capital cycle analysis seeks opportunities both among high return-on-capital companies, benefiting from a positive ("top half") capital cycle, and those in a lower-return ("bottom half") universe. Usually, the bottom half offers opportunities for companies that must consolidate to improve profitability. However, there has been a dearth of opportunity in the bottom half, because very low interest rates have allowed "zombie" companies to borrow at minimal cost and continue operating. The rise in interest rates since 2021–2022 has incrementally generated some of the "creative destruction," which has been lacking in markets since the Global Financial Crisis and creates bottom-half opportunities for Marathon London. However, the lag between interest rate changes and economic impact varies and can be several years. We are beginning to see signs that the unprecedented global tightening cycle of the past few years is impacting companies. The interest cycle has turned in many countries, however, with rates being cut recently. Nevertheless, due to the lag in impact, we expect to see increased opportunity in the bottom half in the coming years.

Marathon is also cognizant of the currently heightened levels of market volatility. While our long-term approach does not seek to rapidly trade in and out of positions, it is possible that ongoing uncertainty will present opportunities to buy into assets at depressed valuations or sell holdings at high valuations.

Harbor Diversified International All Cap Fund

Investment Philosophy:

The Harbor Diversified International All Cap Fund invests primarily in common and preferred stocks of foreign companies, including those in emerging markets. The Fund seeks long-term opportunities driven by capital cycles or supply-side conditions, using a fundamental, bottom-up approach. The Fund typically invests in companies across Europe, Japan, the Pacific Basin and emerging markets, and North America. While inherently diversified, a bias towards smaller and mid-cap businesses in niche industries, coupled with a particularly long holding period, result in a portfolio that is significantly differentiated from the Fund's benchmark index.

data.title: USD Extends Its Lead As Fed Cuts Price Out: USD/JPY To New Cycle Highs
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Despite yesterday's more optimistic US CPI report, markets are now focusing on a bleaker inflationary picture ahead. A major theme in markets is the progressive repricing for a more persistent and damaging US-Iran-Israel war, which would have long-lasting consequences on oil prices.

As you can see from the Polymarket odds, the odds for a 5- to 6-week conflict fell from 80% to the current 47%, adding to the (justified) fears of a damaging long war.

With the Iranian regime holding out stronger than expected, nominating a new leader in Mojtaba Khamenei and continuing its attacks on neighboring countries, it seems that it would either require more force or a new plan to really materialize the conflict into the desired regime change outcome.

Participants and the general media are now roasting the Trump Administration for its lack of exit plans. To me, this could be a strategy to create doubt, as we are "only" on the 13th day of the conflict, but people's skepticism regarding Trump's temerity is justified.

Even his prior advisors warned of such, warning of a "self-inflicted wound" to the US economy as oil rallies back to $95.

For those who haven't looked back yet, the consecutive oil crises in the 1970s were the prelude to the infamous stagflation era, and the market never jokes around with such economic trends.

If anybody is enjoying this at least, it's the dollar bulls, as the petrodollar has been fuming higher since the beginning of the conflict. Rate traders have now priced out around 40 bps of rate cut pricing since the beginning of the conflict, with rate cut odds towards the end of 2026 only around 23 bps.

Subject to an almost 1-to-1 correlation with oil since early March, the greenback has kept outperforming major forex currencies, in particular the subject of this afternoon's FX outlook, the yen.

We will dive into a two-timeframe outlook for USD/JPY as the pair comes close to the 160.00 barrier. An intervention could be near!

USD/JPY Daily Chart

USD/JPY has been the most affected major pair since the beginning of the conflict, holding an almost perfectly negative correlation to the oil prices. Asian countries are the major victims of Strait of Hormuz disruptions, particularly concerning oil deliveries.

Despite Japan having some of the most ample strategic oil reserves, the yen was already facing some heavy scrutiny regarding heavy imports and their more elevated costs. The effect of higher forecasted deficits doesn't help. So if the Hormuz Crisis lasts longer, it could be a final nail in the coffin for the pair.

Furthermore, the Japanese yen is highly correlated to US Treasuries, which have been getting ransacked from the higher inflation expectations due to Japan being the No. 1 owner of the debt asset. The combination of both has led to the current 4.60% move higher in the major pair.

160.00 is the level to watch, as the Ministry of Finance could really move the pin on the numerous threats of an FX intervention. Watch reactions as we regain the January highs.

USD/JPY 1H Chart and Technical Levels

Despite the staggering run in the pair, some profit-taking is unrolling just after the pair wicked above January highs to 159.420. Reversing around the mid-line of the textbook bull channel, particularly at current trading levels could bring interesting mean-reversion opportunities.

If the pair continues back above 159.50 and makes a 4H close above, the idea would get pushed back until 160.00, with potential intervention coming up at these levels.

Levels to place on your USD/JPY charts:

Support Levels:

  • Bull channel lows: 158.10
  • Dec highs major pivot 157.40 to 157.65
  • 156.00 pivotal support
  • 153.50 to 154.00 minor support
  • 146.00 August range main support

Resistance Levels:

  • Daily and 2026 highs: 159.420
  • 159.00 to 159.50 2026 major resistance
  • April 2024: 160.00 to 160.40 major resistance
  • June 2024 mini resistance 160.70 to 161.00
